sinners.name: Dante

literarySources.specificConnection: Dante is the protagonist of The Divine Comedy, making this the most direct literary mapping in the entire franchise. His role as a manager guiding Sinners mirrors Dante Alighieri being guided through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise by Virgil and Beatrice. Every aspect of his design nods to the original: his name (Durante, the poet's birth name), the Cantos (mimicking the 100 cantos of the poem), and the numbering of Sinners mirroring circles of Hell.

The Odyssey provides a structural parallel — Odysseus's long journey home after the Trojan War mirrors Dante's quest to collect the Golden Boughs and reach the Star. Both are Odyssean journeys through hostile territory, and both protagonists require a guide (Vergilius for Dante, Athena's divine intervention for Odysseus). The connection is further reinforced by the Outis deception in Canto IX.

The Golden Boughs are a direct reference to The Aeneid — in the myth, Aeneas must find and pluck a Golden Bough to safely enter and return from the Underworld. In Limbus Company, Dante uses the Golden Boughs to navigate the City's branches and resonate with the Sinners' memories. Vergilius's role as guide mirrors the guidance Aeneas receives, and the concept of a destined journey through a hostile world echoes across all three texts.

Outis means Nobody in Ancient Greek — when Odysseus blinds the Cyclops Polyphemus, he tells the other Cyclopes his name is Outis. When Polyphemus calls for help saying No-man blinded him, no one comes. In Canto IX, Dante encounters the same deception — the mysterious Outis helmet, a direct reference to the original trick. Dante's headless state (a clock replacing their face) mirrors the "no one" interpretation — someone neither fully alive nor dead, trapped between worlds, guided through the City as Dante Alighieri was guided through the afterlife.

sinners.loreSummary: Dante is the manager of Limbus Company's bus, responsible for guiding twelve Sinners through fixed LCB routes to recover lost memories and locate the Golden boughs. He is characterized by his sharp tongue, dramatic flair, and an enigmatic relationship with Vergilius. A central mystery surrounds his true nature — whether he is truly headless, and if so, what that means for his existence.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Dante is the narrative anchor for all twelve Sinners — every character in Limbus Company represents a literary masterpiece, and Dante is the one who manages these stories. Named directly after Dante Alighieri, his design is layered with references to the Divine Comedy: his full name (Durante, the poet's actual birth name), the Canto structure mirroring the poem's 100 cantos, and the Sinners' numbering echoing circles of Hell. Project Moon inverts the Divine Comedy's dynamic — where Dante Alighieri was guided through the afterlife by Virgil and Beatrice, Limbus Company's Dante guides twelve Sinners through the City's branches. Vergilius serves as Dante's own Virgil, a protector and guide in a role reversal of the original. The Aeneid connection is woven through the Golden Boughs themselves — Aeneas must find a Golden Bough to enter the Underworld, just as Dante uses the Golden Boughs to resonate with the Sinners' memories. The Outis connection ties to Dante's headless state — a clock replacing their face symbolizes the "winding" of time, resurrection at the cost of pain, and the Contrapasso concept (poetic justice) where Dante feels the collective pain of every Sinner's death. Dante has no identities or EGOs — instead, his power manifests through the Golden Bough contract system, telepathy, resurrection, and various Sapling of Light abilities.

cantos.summary: Dante's past personality revealed — snarky and aggressive before wiping their own memory and replacing their head with a prosthetic clock.
Gregor's focus — Dante manages the bus and guides the team through their first real mission.
Rodion's focus — Dante observes Rodya's moral conflict with the Yurodiviye.
Sinclair's focus — Dante's encouragement of Sinclair is delayed and awkward; a turning point in Dante's management style.
Yi Sang's focus — Dante manages the team through the dungeon and observes Meursault's peculiar behavior.
Ishmael's focus — Dante refines leadership as Ishmael's frenzy arc peaks and she confronts Captain Ahab.
Heathcliff's focus — Dante observes Heathcliff choose love over mission objective.
Don Quixote's focus — Dante uses the Superbia ability to strengthen Don Quixote; clock hands advance.
Hong Lu's focus — Dante participates in the Xichun rescue plan.
Gregor's past explored — Dante gains Remote Sight ability and explores the Malthael ability.
Current event — Details pending lore progression.

powers.name: Turning Back the Clock
powers.description: Dante can heal injuries and revive Sinners from death by winding back time. The cost is that Dante physically feels how each Sinner died — the pain writhes through their body as time reverses. Dante describes opening a foreboding door filled with the wails of unknown sufferers and pulling the Sinners' hands out from the gateway.

powers.name: Golden Bough Resonance
powers.description: Dante can sense where the Golden Boughs are by "following their star." They perceive the Boughs as shining stars in a swirling sky and can share these visions with the Sinners through their telepathic link.

powers.name: Telepathy
powers.description: Dante communicates only through a ticking sound, altering its frequency and intensity. Only the Sinners and a select few can understand the meaning behind the ticks. When agitated, the speech may include bell chimes and/or steam whistles. Dante can selectively address one Sinner or the entire group at once.

powers.name: Distortion Speech
powers.description: Dante can understand Distortions who have lost the ability to speak human language, extending to their subordinate entities. Acting as interpreter, they relay invaluable information to the Sinners — possibly due to resonance with the Golden Boughs.

powers.name: Remote Sight
powers.description: Gained during the events of Canto IX. Dante can watch over Sinners separated from them, though cannot verbally communicate. They can also "lend strength" and revive distant Sinners.

powers.name: Sapling of Light: Pigritia
powers.description: In Canto VI, Dante creates a field that slows everything except the Sinners within a certain radius. In game, this halves the Speed of all enemies, rounded down.

powers.name: Sapling of Light: Superbia
powers.description: In Canto VII, Dante appears to strengthen the power of a Sinner. In game, this allows an E.G.O. Skill to be slotted without spending any E.G.O Resources.

powers.name: Sapling of Light: Morositas
powers.description: In Canto VIII, Dante uses the strength-averaging aspect of their contract to bring an enemy down to the Sinners' level. In game, this sets the Offense and Defense levels of all units to the average of all units' values for three turns.

powers.name: Sapling of Light: Ira
powers.description: In Canto IX, Dante uses the strength-enhancing aspect of their contract to drastically raise a Sinner's capabilities for the duration of the fight. In game: unbreakable coins, no stagger, minimum HP fixed to 10, and base attacks heal HP. All effects last one turn.

powers.name: Last Resort
powers.description: A preventative measure to prevent the Golden Boughs from falling into wrong hands. By inputting a sequence into the back of their clock head, Dante can self-detonate a charge hot enough to "melt even the Stars of the City." The Golden Boughs cannot survive this explosion.

powers.name: Golden Bough Synchronization
powers.description: Used once during Canto 6-48 to perform an otherwise impossible feat with the help of a Golden Bough, leaving it "burnt" afterwards.

powers.name: Immunity to Memory Wiping
powers.description: Dante can resist memory removal from various sources. They retained their memory of K Corp.'s Singularity after the wipe at the end of Canto IV, and retain memories of characters erased from existence, such as Catherine (Canto VI) and Ren and Sora (Relic Arayashiki effect).

sinners.name: Faust

literarySources.specificConnection: Faust is the protagonist of Goethe's two-part tragic play (1808/1832), a scholar who makes a pact with the devil Mephistopheles trading his soul for infinite knowledge and experience. Limbus Faust mirrors this: her manipulations of the Index, her deals with other factions, and her role as a collector of forbidden knowledge all stem from Faust's unquenchable drive for understanding — even at the cost of others.

Mephistopheles is a figure who bargains for souls across folklore, legend, and literature beyond Goethe's play alone — the devil's bargain as cultural archetype. Limbus Faust doesn't just reference Goethe's Faust; she embodies the idea of the devil's bargain itself. Her signature manipulation of the Index and her morally ambiguous contracts throughout Limbus Company are the Mephistopheles archetype in action — a figure who sees through human weakness and exploits it with cold precision.

The Sorrows of Young Werther supplies the emotional precursor to Goethe's later Faust: a brilliant mind consumed by longing, self-consciousness, and the inability to live normally within the world around him. Limbus Faust suppresses that emotional volatility behind composure and certainty, but the same dangerous hunger for transcendence still defines her.

sinners.loreSummary: Faust is a mysterious Sinner who claims to have no memory of her past, only goals she intends to achieve. She is knowledgeable about abnormalities, the City, and seemingly the true nature of Limbus Company's operations. She speaks in a formal, detached manner and has been shown to act independently of the other Sinners. Her identity is among the most opaque in the group.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Faust in Goethe's tragedy makes a bargain with the Devil (Mephistopheles) — in exchange for unlimited knowledge and worldly pleasures, he pledges his soul. Limbus Company's Faust has made bargains of her own, with Ayin (in Library of Ruina) and possibly others. Her persistent presence across all three games mirrors Faust's timeless, almost meta-fictional existence. The question of whether she remembers, regrets, or would undo her bargains is the central tension of her character.

identities.displayName: The House of Spiders: The Ring Apprentice

egos.description: [HE] Ardor Blossom Star — Gacha, Wrath affinity

cantos.summary: Introduced as the bus crew's calmest and most informed member, establishing her role as Dante's most unsettling source of answers.
Acts as a detached adviser through the K Corp incident, reinforcing how much she knows about the City while revealing little about herself.
The WARP Train incident exposes unusual vulnerability when her access to the Gesellschaft is disrupted, without making the story a dedicated Faust canto.
Remains one of the crew's most composed strategists during Hong Lu's family conflict, continuing the pattern of influence without a primary focus canto.

sinners.name: Ishmael

literarySources.specificConnection: The sole survivor of a whaling ship destroyed by the white whale Moby Dick, Ishmael narrates the obsessive pursuit of the whale by Captain Ahab. His survival and transformation through the experience mirrors Limbus Ishmael's arc — she alone survives the confrontation with Captain Ahab and is fundamentally changed by it. Ishmael's role as the narrator and sole survivor connects directly to Limbus Ishmael's identity as the one who remembers and bears witness.

Captain Wolf of The Sea-Wolf is Ahab's literary foil — a brutal survivalist whose philosophical strength is undercut by his own hidden refinement (he writes poetry in secret). Both Wolf and Ahab are captains whose obsession reveals something essential about human nature under pressure. Ishmael survives both encounters and in both cases is changed by the experience — the literary survivor archetype that connects London to Melville through Ishmael's eyes.

sinners.loreSummary: Ishmael is an experienced sailor with a long history in the City, having sailed on numerous vessels including the PM regular ship L'Manhattan. She is pragmatic, cynical, and initially distant from the other Sinners. Her Canto reveals a deep connection to Captain Bram and the events aboard the Pequod, driving a personal arc of confronting rather than fleeing her past.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: In Herman Melville's Moby-Dick, Ishmael is the sole survivor of the whaling ship Pequod, which is destroyed after Captain Ahab's obsessive pursuit of the white whale. Ishmael narrates the story, making him both witness and survivor. Limbus Company's Ishmael parallels this — she alone survived whatever happened on the Pequod-equivalent voyage, and her journey in Canto 7 is about whether she will repeat Ahab's mistake (vengeance leading to destruction) or choose differently.

identities.displayName: The House of Spiders: The Middle Apprentice

egos.description: [ZAYIN] Hundred-Footed Death Maggot — Battle Pass (paid), Gloom affinity

cantos.summary: Takes a harsh blow for Dante — demonstrates willingness to protect colleagues despite prior complaints about management.
Falls into neurosis when forced back to the Great Lake waters where her crew died — devotes herself ceaselessly to preparations.
Focus character — propensity for frenzy, excessive anger, violence, instability, and obsession peaks as she confronts Captain Ahab.

sinners.name: Sinclair

literarySources.specificConnection: Emil Sinclair is a young man caught between two worlds — the safe light of conventional morality and a dark 'other side' represented by Max Demian. His awakening to his own shadow, and his journey toward selfhood through confrontation with that darkness, is the direct template for Limbus Sinclair's arc from sheltered innocence to confronting the monstrous power within himself.

Both Sinclair and Dmitri Karamazov are young men destroyed and remade by exposure to darkness. Dmitri's collision between base impulses and moral aspiration, his guilt, and his spiritual crisis mirror Sinclair's journey from innocence through the Klein family dark wing to self-acceptance. Both Hesse and Dostoevsky use the 'descent to find truth' structural arc — entering the shadow to emerge transformed.

sinners.loreSummary: Sinclair is the youngest Sinner, initially appearing soft, bookish, and deeply uncomfortable with violence. His arc across Cantos 1–4 charts a violent transformation — from a child who believed in light to someone who has seen and committed dark acts. He struggles with guilt and the fear that he is not the person he thought he was. His EGO 'Nobles' is both literally and figuratively his darker self made manifest.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: In Demian, Sinclair is a young boy who is欺负ed by a bully named Kromer. He is rescued and introduced to a deeper worldview by Demian, who teaches him that the world is not divided simply into light and dark — that both exist in all people, and that becoming whole requires accepting one's shadow. Limbus Sinclair's arc follows this almost exactly: Kromer's bullying forces him to confront his darker potential, and his 'Nobles' EGO represents his awakening to the darker half of himself. His eventual acceptance of both sides is his metamorphosis.

identities.displayName: The House of Spiders: The Pinky Apprentice

egos.description: [ZAYIN] Branch of Knowledge — Base, Gluttony affinity
[TETH] Impending Day — Battle Pass (free), Wrath affinity
[WAW] Tears of the Tarnished Blood — Battle Pass (free), Gluttony affinity

cantos.summary: Focus character — demonstrates rougher side capable of grudges, violence, and disdain; confronts Kromer and develops self-confidence.
Empathy particularly prominent — assists Ishmael during the Ahab confrontation.

sinners.name: Don Quixote

literarySources.specificConnection: Alonso Quijano believes he is a knight-errant and rides out to right wrongs, mistaking windmills for giants and peasant women for noble ladies. His unwavering conviction and his golden dream — that the world can be made right through heroic action — are the heart of Cervantes's novel. Limbus Don Quixote's EGO manifestations and relentless pursuit of her ideals follow this template directly.

Tartuffe weaponizes religious conviction — he performs righteousness while committing fraud. Don Quixote inverts this: genuine conviction mistaken for madness. Limbus Don Quixote's unwavering belief in her golden dream parallels both Tartuffe's performed faith and Quixote's genuine delusion — exploring the gap between performed righteousness and actual conviction. The performance vs. reality of virtue theme connects directly across both works.

sinners.loreSummary: Don Quixote is an exuberant, slightly unhinged Sinner with a fiercely moral worldview. She believes the world is full of evil that must be vanquished and has a habit of charging into dangerous situations with absolute conviction. Her optimism is both her greatest strength and her most dangerous flaw — she genuinely cannot see the world as it is, and this refusal to be cynical is both admirable and costly.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Cervantes's Don Quixote is a man who, having read too many chivalric romances, loses his grip on reality and rides out to fight giants (which are, in reality, windmills). He is both comedic and tragic — his delusions are symptoms of a profound dissatisfaction with the modern world, and his sincerity makes him sympathetic despite his absurdity. Limbus Don Quixote operates the same way: her worldview is objectively delusional, but her moral conviction is real and costs her personally. The absurdity is not in her lack of sincerity but in the world's refusal to match it.

identities.displayName: Lobotomy E.G.O:: In the Name of Love and Hate
The Index Proxy -  Effloresced E.G.O:: Procuration

egos.displayName: In the Name of Love and Hate

cantos.summary: Impulsive desire to protect others causes unnecessary conflict during the LCB's attempt to enter K Corp.'s Nest.
One of her '863 dreams' fulfilled — sailing the Great Lake in U Corp.'s District.
Focus character — true identity as Sancho revealed; backstory as former Bloodfiend of La Manchaland explored; reconciles Sancho self with Don Quixote persona.
Current focus — Don Quixote's role in the current seasonal transition.

sinners.name: Ryoshu

literarySources.specificConnection: Yoshihide, the painter in Akutagawa's Hell Screen, is obsessed with painting the perfect depiction of hell — and achieves it by burning his daughter alive as his final act. His name becomes Ryoshu's directly. His philosophy — that art demands the ultimate sacrifice, that aesthetic truth justifies any cost — is the foundation of Limbus Ryoshu's 'Art is everything' worldview. The screen of hell he produces is both masterpiece and atrocity.

Dorian Gray's portrait bears his sins while he remains beautiful — art externalizing moral corruption. Ryoshu treats art as more real than reality, using violence and madness as raw material. Her EGO and identity art literally manifests her inner world externally. Both characters use art to externalize and hide their true nature simultaneously — the portrait and the painting as moral mirrors. The art as corruption vehicle theme is shared across both Wilde and Akutagawa.

Akutagawa's In a Grove turns murder into layered testimony, with every witness shaping reality to protect pride, desire, or shame. Ryoshu approaches violence the same way: not as a stable fact, but as a composition that changes depending on who frames it. The story's fractured truth tradition fits her habit of treating atrocity as interpretation.

sinners.loreSummary: Ryoshu is an artist who sees the world in terms of compositions — beauty, violence, and meaning. She is aloof, sardonic, and deeply unconventional, often making cryptic statements about what she finds 'beautiful' or 'composed correctly.' Her art is literal in the City — she literally manifests her paintings as combat abilities. Her emotional range appears narrow, but she is not unfeeling; she simply processes the world through an aesthetic lens that others find disturbing.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Ryoshu draws most directly from Akutagawa's Hell Screen, where artistic perfection is inseparable from cruelty, but her wider literary frame also includes Akutagawa's fascination with fractured testimony and moral ugliness. In a Grove matters because Ryoshu never treats violence as neutral fact; she reframes it, edits it, and turns it into an aesthetic statement. The violence in her art echoes the brutal imagery of Edo-period woodblock prints, where beauty and gore coexist in the same frame. Project Moon's version keeps that tension intact: she is genuinely talented, genuinely dangerous, and genuinely indifferent to conventional morality.

egos.description: [WAW] Great Trichiliocosm (三千大世界) — Battle Pass (paid), Wrath affinity

cantos.summary: Present — described as mysterious and calm, sketching while others panic.
Accepts an EGO gift from Yi Sang; cryptic dialogue throughout.

sinners.name: Meursault

literarySources.specificConnection: Meursault is Camus's exemplar of the absurd man — he feels no grief at his mother's funeral, commits a motiveless murder, and refuses to perform the emotions society demands at his trial. His refusal to invest social rituals with meaning, and his ultimate acceptance of the indifference of the universe, is Limbus Meursault's direct template.

Meursault's refusal to perform emotions or invest social rituals with meaning is structurally identical to Vladimir and Estragon's predicament in Waiting for Godot — going through meaningless motions, unable to act, waiting for something that never arrives. Meursault is the absurd man who doesn't wait for Godot; he simply is. His trial demands he perform meaning he doesn't feel — exactly the trap Vladimir and Estragon are in. Both works interrogate why people perform rituals when no ultimate meaning exists.

sinners.loreSummary: Meursault is characterized by his extreme emotional flatness — he rarely speaks beyond monosyllables, shows little visible reaction to events that would distress others, and is completely literal in his thinking. He is not cruel, but his lack of emotional response makes him unpredictable. His Canto reveals the cause of this detachment and forces him to confront a reckoning he has been avoiding.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Albert Camus's The Stranger opens with the iconic line: 'Mother died today. Or, maybe, yesterday — I can't be sure.' Meursault's narration is emotionally detached, describing events with clinical precision. He is tried and executed not for killing a man (the crime) but for not crying at his mother's funeral — for failing to perform the social emotions that society expects. Limbus Meursault has this same quality of existing outside the emotional scripts others follow. His literary connection is not just thematic — it is structural: his Canto is structured around a trial, a reckoning, and an ending that forces meaning onto what Meursault himself experienced as meaninglessness.

egos.description: [ZAYIN] Chain of Others — Base, Pride affinity

cantos.summary: Noted for reciting 'irrelevant emoticons' while reading an email aloud — peculiar behavior observed by the team.
Mentions wandering 'in search of life's joys'; assists during the Ahab confrontation.
Weighs mission success against Heathcliff's safety — ultimately chooses Heathcliff's survival over the Golden Bough.
Past explored: activities deemed 'right' and 'wrong' by society; time at N Corp; familiarity with Vespa Crabro.
Fails to report the sudden disappearance of Limbus Company's target.
Childhood described — mother scolded him similarly to how Outis reprimands others.

sinners.name: Hong Lu

literarySources.specificConnection: Hong Lu comes from a wealthy, powerful family whose decline he witnesses and ultimately leaves behind. His calm detachment from suffering and his role as a witness to collapse mirrors the novel's themes of impermanence and the Buddhist cycle of suffering. His placid acceptance of the team's suffering and his quiet helpfulness are the behavior of someone watching a great house fall.

Both Dream of the Red Chamber and Journey to the West feature groups bound by fate, each with distinct personalities, navigating supernatural beings and corrupt institutions. Hong Lu's placid acceptance of suffering and calm helpfulness mirrors Tripitaka's serene moral leadership, while his detachment parallels Sun Wukong's irreverent freedom. Both stories use the group-traveling-through-a-hostile-world structure where each character reveals their nature through crisis.

sinners.loreSummary: Hong Lu is polite, soft-spoken, and deferential to others — he speaks in a formal, archaic register that sets him apart from the other Sinners. He has a habit of smiling even in disturbing situations. His Canto explores his family background and the rot beneath the surface of his seemingly gentle demeanor. He is deeply aware of the corruption around him and chooses to participate minimally rather than fight it.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Dream of the Red Chamber follows the decline of the Jia family — a wealthy, powerful household sustained by connections and corruption rather than merit. It is a novel about the decay of aristocratic systems, the entrapment of individuals within family structures, and the futility of trying to maintain appearances as the foundation crumbles. Limbus Hong Lu inherits this thematic core: he is trapped by his family name, aware of the rot, and too pragmatic (or too fatalistic) to resist. His smile is the most unsettling thing about him — it is both the mask of a child trained to perform pleasantness and a genuine expression of acceptance of a world he knows is doomed.

identities.displayName: The House of Spiders: The Ring Nursefather

egos.displayName: Tears of the Tarnished Blood [汚血泣淚]

egos.description: [WAW] Tears of the Tarnished Blood — Battle Pass (paid), Gluttony affinity
[HE] To Remain Oneself — Collab, Pride affinity

cantos.summary: Takes the role of Young-ji in the League of Nine Littérateurs play; demonstrates emotional maturity and insight into Yi Sang's mental state.
Empathetic and delicate side further developed through interactions with other characters.
Character depth continues to build.
Focus character — backstory and family explored.

sinners.name: Heathcliff

literarySources.specificConnection: Heathcliff is an orphan brought to Wuthering Heights who develops an all-consuming, destructive love for Catherine Earnshaw. His revenge against those who separated them consumes both him and the next generation. His elemental, almost supernatural passion and his connection to the natural landscape of the Yorkshire moors are the direct template for Limbus Heathcliff — a man whose love and rage are indistinguishable, and whose violence is the expression of both.

Brontë opens Wuthering Heights with an epigraph from Burns's poem 'Highland Mary,' and Heathcliff's character is written in Burns's Romantic grammar: 'O my Luve is like a red, red rose,' 'forlorn, torn.' Burns's celebration of elemental passion, nature as emotional landscape, and love as overwhelming force is the atmosphere Heathcliff breathes. His moorland love for Catherine is the Romantic hero from a Burns poem given narrative form — impulsive, fierce, and ultimately destructive.

sinners.loreSummary: Heathcliff is hot-tempered, prone to violence when pushed, and harbors deep resentment toward those he perceives as privileged or cruel. He is fiercely loyal to those he considers 'his people' and will go to extreme lengths to protect or avenge them. His Canto reveals that this fury has specific roots — a history of class-based abuse and betrayal that created the wound he carries.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: In Wuthering Heights, Heathcliff is a foundling taken in by Mr. Earnshaw, who bestows great affection on him — to the intense resentment of Hindley Earnshaw, who sees Heathcliff as a usurper of his birthright. Hindley's abuse turns Heathcliff from a spirited child into a vengeful adult who systematically destroys both the Earnshaw and Linton families. The novel asks whether Heathcliff's vengeance is justified (it destroys cruel people) or monstrous (it destroys innocent people too). Limbus Heathcliff asks the same question: his rage is earned, but what he does with it may not be.

identities.displayName: W Corp. L4 Cleanup Agent - CCA

cantos.summary: Focus character — processes trauma surrounding Catherine; shifts from vengeful anger to purposeful remembrance; weapon inscription changes from 'REVENGE' to 'REmember'.
Opens up to Dante and Don Quixote about his situation.

sinners.name: Yi Sang

literarySources.specificConnection: Yi Sang is named after the pen name of Korean author Kim Hyeok (pen name: Yi Sang, 1907–1937), whose works — especially the novel The Flight of Earth (iqug-ui Han-Yang) and the short story 'Fog' (Angae) — deal with themes of illness, existential despair, dissolution of the self, and the inability to love. Limbus Yi Sang is a poet dealing with a similar condition — a metaphorical 'fog' that obscures his connection to himself and others.

The name 'Yi Sang' is Chinese-Korean: 'Yi' (里/Earth) and 'Sang' (哀/Grief/Mourn) — literally 'Earthly Grief.' This etymology connects to Dream of the Red Chamber's themes of family decay and the mourning of a lost world, while Yi Sang's lung disease (literary: author Yi Sang died of tuberculosis; in Limbus: related to his abilities) mirrors the consumption-as-metaphor tradition in both the novel and Eastern literature broadly.

The Wings is one of Yi Sang's clearest portraits of dissociation: a narrator trapped between paralysis, humiliation, and the fantasy of escape. That split self is central to Limbus Yi Sang. His quiet affect and poetic detachment are not calm so much as a survival posture, mirroring the story's sense of a mind observing its own collapse from a distance.

sinners.loreSummary: Yi Sang is quiet, introspective, and physically unwell — he coughs frequently and is often seen writing in a notebook. He is the group's resident poet, and his poetry serves as both a coping mechanism and a lens through which he processes the world. He is the most philosophically withdrawn of the Sinners, seemingly accepting of his own deterioration. His Canto confronts the origins of his condition and whether his art justifies or merely documents his suffering.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Real-world Yi Sang (1907-1937) was a Korean author whose literary career lasted only a decade before tuberculosis killed him at 30. His work is characterized by extreme emotional intensity, a focus on death and decay, and a recurring motif of the self dissolving into fog or absence. The Flight of Earth provides the cosmically exhausted side of the character, while The Wings adds the intimate, dissociative side: a mind trapped in self-observation, humiliation, and fantasy. Limbus Yi Sang is built from both. His fog is both illness and art: impenetrable, obscuring, and strangely beautiful.

identities.displayName: The House of Spiders: The Index Nursefather

egos.description: [ZAYIN] Crows's Eye View — Base, Sloth affinity
[WAW] Great Trichiliocosm (三千大世界) — Battle Pass (free), Wrath affinity

cantos.summary: Focus character — depressive state, suicidal thoughts, and the dissolution of the League of Nine Littérateurs revealed.
Jokingly accused by Ishmael and Hong Lu of mocking a love letter Heathcliff received.
Addressed as 'Ha Yung' by Gubo.

sinners.name: Rodion

literarySources.specificConnection: Rodya Romanov commits murder believing his intelligence exempts him from moral law — and is destroyed by guilt and the weight of his deed. His psychological spiral, his need for external validation, and his desperate grasping for a way to make his suffering mean something are Limbus Rodion's direct template. Both characters are physically imposing men of the people who are destroyed by an act committed partly from desperation and partly from pride.

Rodya Romanov and Dmitri Karamazov share the archetype of the passionate man falsely accused, destroyed by circumstance and impulse. Both are physically imposing men of the people, prone to impulsiveness, carrying enormous guilt over something not fully under their control. The psychological spiral and the desperate need for redemption through suffering is shared across both Dostoevsky masterworks. Rodion's anxious self-talk and need for validation is pure Dmitri.

sinners.loreSummary: Rodion is upbeat, charming, and constantly scheming — she presents herself as a 'big shot' who is destined for greatness and isn't afraid to cut corners to get there. Beneath this bravado, however, is a sharp intelligence that is working through a personal theory about who deserves what in the world. Her Canto tests this theory against reality and forces her to confront the cost of living by it.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Dostoevsky's Raskolnikov invents a theory: great men (Napoleon, Lycurgus) are above conventional morality — they can transgress laws because their ends justify their means. He tests this theory by murdering a pawnbroker. The novel is not really about the murder — it is about the psychological consequences of believing that some people are permitted to do terrible things 'for the greater good.' Limbus Rodion has a similar theory, and her arc tests it. The 'punishment' in the title refers not to the legal consequences but to the self-destruction that Raskolnikov brings upon himself through guilt. Rodion's arc will likely follow the same trajectory.

identities.displayName: Lobotomy E.G.O:: The Sword Sharpened with Tears

egos.displayName: Tears of the Tarnished Blood [汚血泣淚]

egos.description: [ZAYIN] What is Cast — Base, Pride affinity
[TETH] Rime Shank — Battle Pass (free), Gloom affinity
[HE] 4th Match Flame — Battle Pass (paid), Wrath affinity
[TETH] Effervescent Corrosion — Battle Pass (paid), Gluttony affinity

cantos.summary: Focus character — backstory with the Yurodiviye and District 25 explored; struggles with guilt tied to her past with her friend Sonya and a tragedy in her hometown.

sinners.name: Outis

literarySources.specificConnection: 'Outis' is Greek for 'nobody' — Odysseus's self-introduction to the Cyclops Polyphemus, which blinds the Cyclops. When Polyphemus tells the other Cyclopes that 'nobody' hurt him, they don't come to help. Odysseus's 'nobody' trick is one of the most famous examples of identity deception in Western literature, and Limbus Outis uses a similar deception — her real name and identity are deliberately obscured.

Outis shares the Divine Comedy literary reference with Dante — in The Divine Comedy, Odysseus (rendered as 'Ulysses' in the Italian) appears in the Eighth Circle of Hell for his role in the Trojan Horse deception. Outis's connection to both the Odyssey and the Divine Comedy places her at the intersection of two texts that deal with deception, identity, and the cost of cunning.

sinners.loreSummary: Outis is the most recently revealed Sinner in Limbus Company, and her true identity is a significant plot point. She presents as a reliable, somewhat cold strategist who prioritizes group efficiency. Her personal Canto reveals layers of deception around her name, her history, and her motivations. She is the Sinner whose identity is most explicitly tied to lying about who she is.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Odysseus in Homer's Odyssey is defined by his cunning (Greek: mētis) — he is 'the man of twists and turns.' His signature achievement (the Trojan Horse) and his greatest failure (blinding Polyphemus) both depend on deception about his identity. In Dante's Inferno, Ulysses is consigned to the Eighth Circle for fraud — specifically for the Trojan Horse deception. Outis as a name is a meta-joke: 'I am nobody' said the man who was most definitely somebody. Limbus Outis plays the same game — her false identity conceals someone real, and the revelation of who she actually is has implications for the LCB's larger mission.

egos.displayName: I'll Go fer Scissors. How 'Bout You?

sinners.name: Gregor

literarySources.specificConnection: Gregor Samsa wakes one morning to find himself transformed into a monstrous vermin — losing his job, his identity, and eventually his family's care. His silent, persistent humanity beneath the bug form and his slow erasure from the family's consciousness is Limbus Gregor's direct template. Both Gregors are men made monstrous by circumstance, who lose the ability to speak and must watch as the world moves on without them.

Kafka's letters — especially Letters to Milena — explore the impossibility of making contact when language feels inadequate. Gregor's transformation isolates him from communication: he can hear others clearly but cannot make himself understood. This mirrors Kafka's letters, written from within an impossible situation, reaching toward connection that cannot arrive. Gregor's silent humanity is the same impulse as Kafka's letters — reaching through the impossibility.

sinners.loreSummary: Gregor is a friendly, somewhat self-deprecating man who has lost his left arm and replaced it with a prosthetic 'bug arm' — a living, insectoid limb that he can transform into various weapons. He is the most 'everyman' of the Sinners in personality, which makes his physical transformation (the bug arm) all the more stark. His Canto explores the circumstances of losing his arm and the social stigma of being visibly 'other' in the City.
sinners.literaryConnectionNotes: Kafka's The Metamorphosis opens with Gregor's transformation, which he initially accepts with more equanimity than his horrified family. The story is less about the horror of transformation than about how the transformed person is treated by those around them — Gregor becomes an inconvenience, then a burden, and is ultimately treated as something less than human. Limbus Gregor inherits this social dimension: his bug arm marks him as 'different,' and his Canto deals with how society treats those who are visibly changed. The arm itself — a living thing grafted onto a human body — also connects to the Project Moon universe's obsession with technology, abnormality, and the boundary between human and machine.

cantos.summary: Focus character — background revealed; insectile bio-prosthetic arm; complicated relationship with mother Hermann.
Central figure — undergoes 'Roach Emperor' transformation; past with Hermann comes to the forefront.
